speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Fannie Cuthbert Byers has lived all her life near Viola. She recalls staying in the Comer's Hotel so she could attend school, the young arsonist who burnt the church, school, and hall, and the coming of the Seventh-day Adventist to Viola. Other memories include the Inland Train, Packing Apples, and Juliaetta, the first wheat she ever saw harvested, and how her father prospered during the 1893 Depression.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: The First Thing I Remember Our sister who's passed away now, she married a man who come from Wisconsin to a millwright at Potlatch. He worked there, and they got married and had a house. I can remember it just as well, one of the little, it was a company town man. and everybody had the little square houses. And I used to stay over there quite a lot. And on Sunday we'd get up and walk up to Princeton, walk the railroad track just to be going someplace. I don't remember that, well, I suppose Potlatch did have a school right, maybe not right then, but very soon in a church or two. And then our father used to buy and sell cattle and we used to sell at the meal, I mean at the store, that was a company store and they bought everything. I can remember even washing parsnips in the spring, you know, our dad raised a good garden and parsnips was pretty, that was something special, you know, everybody didn't have them. And we'd sell them but the Gunny sacks, parsnips in the spring. Well, that's about the last I remember about Potlatch and it got to be a quite a big town. But I don't think people at all probably they did shop there, but seems like it's like it is nowadays. People go to someplace else to buy clothes and things, you know, they say people...

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, pretty good, don't you remember Jack, that man worked in the shoe store that shot himself? Comerford, Jack Comerford?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: He married a girl, a brown girl, and I don't know, I guess they were having trouble anyway, I can remember that was quite an ordeal when he shot himself in one night in the in the shoe store.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that in Potlatch?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Uh-huh, uh-huh. But old McDonald for years had the Potlatch store and he was an old Scotsman that was a friend of my dad's and he used to come out and visit and buy cattle and then my dad would put them and deliver. But it was a general store, it hit everything but maybe not so much of a... probably working man's clothes more than people's. Probably the women didn't have much fine with them days, but they had to have shoes and and good clothes for the men who worked in the woods, that's about what I can remember.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And then a few years later we went to the dance hall was up over the store, I've been there lots of times to dances and I don't know if that was what's I-O-O-F, what that night's a Columbus, what is it, what it's a lodge but what is it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: That's not else, no, but it was something, a lodge that I can remember had the, I had the hall.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: That was the dance hall?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Mm-hmm, upstairs.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: So and then I don't know much about the schools but they have had good schools there now and do, of course that was a good many years ago, that's 60 years ago at least. A lot of things can happen that length of time.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well when you were young here and growing up was Viola called, Viola was it called? No, it's been Viola ever since I, but I can remember the talk before, can't you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: You never remembered anything but four miles.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I think that's when it changed to Viola when they got the post office here because we never got mail and I can remember when we got the mail route, you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No, you wasn't home. See she was a little older than me and about the time I was going to grade school here she was going to high school in Moscow so and then after she got away from high school and grown up then she went several years away from this town that's why she don't, but I'm sure in your time nobody and we never had four miles. It was always Viola but it seemed like when it was plata, platted when they got this it was named four mile and why I don't know, it's four mile creek and I don't know anybody that doesn't know why it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know how Viola, how they got the name of Squeal?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, there's several different, one girl I went to school with, she always claimed that it was named after her or some of these old fellas, but it wasn't. Viola Lynch was, was never lived here when she was, she was a big girl. Yeah, that's what I know and so I don't know why it was named Viola and I don't believe anybody else does, somebody I suppose just picked that name. But anyway, we didn't have the train through here then, the train went through long, long, long, long train. Right. I was 15 or 16 when we had the electric train going through. I can remember that was quite an event when, well, to get down to Deepon, go on the train, you know, it had two or three trains a day. You could go to Moscow and come back on the same time, go to Flus, or even to Spokane and get home the same day because I had so many trains there and then as time went on, just got less and less till we didn't have any.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that a big thing, did the kids go down there much when they were building it? Oh yes, that was a great thing to go down and ride on the, what do you call, things that they worked, the working men get, what are they?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Push cars, hand cars. Yeah, hand cars, hand cars, yeah. But I never got in on that much. I was big enough to, but I lived three miles away. But the kids and the big boys, that was always a great thing. I can remember some of them maybe almost get on the trestle and you had to know what to do if you got caught on the trestle and you didn't dare with a hand car, but a foot you could get off the side, you know, if it didn't put you under. But I think I would always been afraid I'd get caught out there on a hand car. Then you'd be in a bad way, but nobody ever got killed. But I know they used to use the hand cars at night and trips just like they do with a steal in the car nowadays, or probably not just like that, but just fun. But we used to have ball game and viola, on Sundays.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that when you were young?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yeah. And then go down to the depot and see who come in on the train. It wasn't nothing else much to do, you know. We never had a showhouse here or anything.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would anyone special ever come here, particularly?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, when these cabins, one time we had about four cabins to rentable cabins before.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did they they didn't call them motels?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, they were young, immature motels, I would say. They were renting out, you know. Some people would come for the winter and go to school, or if they had a job, why then they'd rent a little cabin. Well, one time old John E. Lewis, you remember?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I never seen him, but he stayed out. Something was happening in town, a convention, and he couldn't get the hotels were full. And he come out to viola and stayed all night in one of those cabins, but very few knew it until it was quite a story afterwards. But nobody hardly knew it was him, only the people that...

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: John Ellis? Yeah, the old, what is he?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: He's a labor leader. He was a union. Yeah, union leader. Well, that was about the only one I remember coming here. But I had a great big car. What was it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: The Duesenberg or something, and we found out later that he spent the night in viola. But, of course, I was going to school. That was probably a... We thought it was a big event. It didn't amount to anything.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Was the Manson sawmill working when you were... All my life. The girls was two Manson girls, and they always done the cooking. And I guess Manson's had a sawmill in about four different halls between here and where I lived at the creek, and logged off all these hills around here. And then the last one they had was on the other side of the mountain. But the last of that family just passed away, just when did Ed die last winter. He married one of my nieces. That was one of the boys.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: But there was a big family, wasn't there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I can remember when they built this house here at the store. That was the Manson house. And that was a pretty good house in its day.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there a story you told me last time that at one time there were more people living around the Manson sawmill than in town here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, that was when we... No, that wasn't the Manson sawmill. That was when I was little. And it was right up the creek here. And my dad used to say that there was about as many... I don't remember it. I remember seeing the sawmill driving by, but he said there was as many up the creek here just in the first mile where the men who worked at the mill. I guess they must have had shacks around, little houses where they worked in the mill. But I just can't quite remember that. Then when I got old enough to remember why it was the Manson sawmill.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: When was the mill in Canada?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Our father come to when he came out here. Well, that was over Flanagan Creek, I think. Over someplace, a Kaufman sawmill was over on Flanagan Creek. He come here from Kansas. Yeah, from Kansas and worked a year and then sent for our mother. And she come out on the train with five kids, she always said. I herded five kids from Kansas. I think Jim and you girls was all. I wasn't born yet. Jim and you girls were all. I wasn't born yet. And then the only brother we had, I told you, he was killed when our father and him was making wood when I was three months old up on our place. This sister who is in Spokane at the home now, she used to always go up on the hill. She remembered the tree where Jim got killed. And this woman who lives right here, she's a widow now. I don't know if you saw her that Mrs. Balmer was a kind of a odd looking. Well, I don't know if she's odd looking. We think she's had a slight stroke. Her dad and my dad come together and they work.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They came out together looking for a place to settle?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Uh-huh. And my dad always said, from Walla Walla up here, there was a lot of railroad land. You know when the railroad went through on either there was a big right away and then later they sold that off. He said he could have filed or bought any place from Walla Walla up to here, the railroad land, but he come on to the woods because he had been hailed out so many times that he didn't want the farmland. So he always wondered if he made a mistake. You know, they, these were just brush ranches, sort of. They would clear off land and he eventually got enough to make a living, but then they did different things. These down here were wheat farmers and sometimes that was pretty good, the wheat farming. But I guess he probably made a living too, just like they did. But he always...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he sell a lot of wood?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Uh-huh. Yes, every winter I can remember somebody was making wood they make it awful cheap, you know, them days. And now it's 40 dollars a cord.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where would they take it? Where would they sell it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: One time, just to... Well, the farmers all had to buy wood. They didn't have timber.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Four horse?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yes. Wagons and... Just that was a great thing in the spring and the fall of four horse wagons coming by wood. And then he'd sell his wood. But I remember one time he said that he bought a new sled in the fall and had his wood made as usual and never gotten... Snow never come enough to make the roads good until way late. He said in March he finally got to Holland with the Pullman, sold all his wood and paid for his sled. He worried all winter that the snow didn't come. You know it used to be we had lots more snow than we do now. I don't know where the snow went to. You could go from... Well, I think cutting all the timber off there was something done away with it or else we just changed because you could go horseback from here to Pullman just over the fences. The drifts would be big and it would get hard. I don't think in the winter they used to even stay on the road lots of times when you're going someplace. Just go cross country. That would be usually in the winter and you could just go across the way. We never here. We used to go to school over the tops of the fences all the time. Yeah. And the crust could support a horse. Yes. Yeah. And I don't know what changed all that. Seems like then when cars come and they got better roads we just stayed on the road. And we always called going to Uncle B's. That was a family. They're not really not uncles. Some of the married relation. But they always exchange Christmas and Thanksgiving. Seems like Aunt Farrah always had Thanksgiving dinner. Well, it was something very strange if we didn't go on the sled. And look now here it should just fall when we have Thanksgiving. But when I was a kid we always figured to go over there on the sled. So things have changed some way or other. I don't know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it colder in the summers or? I thought it was just about the same wasn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Just as hot. Yes. But we just seemed to have more snow. But.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have any more rain? Or were the creeks bigger then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, it seemed like they did because I remember when a little girl was drowned down here. Some of Eunice. No. It was the other hill family. It wasn't Bud's sister.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Was it Bud's sister that was drowned?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I believe it was. There was during our young life it was two hills on either side of the road down here on the farm. No relation. And they each raised big families. And I didn't know what their relation till I grew up. And one was Eunice's husband's family and the other was Frank Hill. And one of them families had a girl who was drowned. Eunice was sister-in-law. Bud's sister. Yeah. Well, it seemed as though they were crossing on a plank or something.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: What was it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I remember the body washed way down below the schoolhouse. Little girls probably six or five. And I remember one time up under this. That's four mile creek. And under the bridge up here, Lou and Otis almost drowned when they were little. So evidently, oh, there was more water before they cut off the timber off.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Could you swim in that creek?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No. Not be swimming. But up here, about a mile up the creek where Ava lives now, Davis has lived there. And in the spring, we used to have a raft there in front of the house, us kids. There's two or three boys and two or three girls. And I was always there. We had a raft in them and we used to go around in the raft. So it used to be big. Yeah. Much bigger than now. But then you read that after the timber's cut off, it does something. They don't hold the water like it used to. So they was a lot bigger. But it seems like, oh, for an awful lot of years, this creek ain't been very big. But this goes clear down to Colfax. You've been down to, you know, this just gets bigger and bigger. I guess it goes to the Columbia River, Donner. I don't know. Eventually. Yes. Well, I don't know about the snakes. I wish one of those would go. Well, some of them maybe do. I don't know. I'd have to look on the map, I guess.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, were there other sawmills around besides?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there a lot of them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Lots. Well, that was big business them days. And I don't know what they did with their lumber. Now where my son works up at the Bennett Mill. You familiar with the Bennett Mill?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I used to build old houses with lumber.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yes. And that Bennett Mill's been remodeled in the last 10 years. But all my young life, that was Boone's Mill, you know. Beth Darling used to cook up there. And it was a big mill in its day, but now it's got to be old, more modern, and a bigger mill. And I know they sell there. A lot of their lumber goes to Japan where my son works now. But I think it just went around here to the town that was built enough. And you know all these big houses that you see now didn't cost so much to build those days. So I guess it was used a lot other around. It wasn't for many people too. No. Moscow wasn't a very big town then.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would they work all year round?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No, but they log different. They, now they do what you call hot log. And the logs are cut and loaded on the trucks and come right to the mill. It used to be they worked in the woods all winter and cut them and decked them up in big piles. And then when the roads got good, if they never got snow to haul them in the winter, then they were hauled with teams on wagons in the summer when the roads got dry. So it's all together different. I don't know which is the better way, but it's the way they done it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did they usually haul with the sleighs around here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yes, Manson, I can remember, used to have sleighs in the winter to haul these logs. Big, oh I don't know, decks four or five. I remember this one I said just died. Ed Manson, he used to, oh he grew up in the lumber business. And he visited us here a few years ago and looked up the mountain and he said, and a better road than used to be. He said, I don't know if I could skin an outfit down there now. And I said, well, when we get old we don't do things.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And he said, who the hell's getting old?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: But he was as old as I was, but he didn't want to think about it, you know. But he used to think he was a pretty good driver with the teens. They always had nice big horses. And Potlatch, Barn, always had the best horses in the country. They did log them with teens too.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, how much later was it they put them on the streams and brought them down the river? Well, I guess maybe they always done that a little after Potlatch got to, they used to have log, what do they call them?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Log drives. Yeah, log drives down the river. But I don't know whether they always did that. I suppose they did. In the spring when the water got high they would deck them on the banks and then roll them in the river. That was quite a thing to go down and see the logs come down the river. So maybe they always done that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You go up to Potlatch and watch that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh no, I never did. Although we see them come down never in the Palouse River. I don't really the head log. Yeah, I think Lewiston's where the log drives were. I don't think this stream would get big enough.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was that story again about the day that you were born?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, that's the sister in Spokane tells that. That the midwife, the mother of all Lady Smith, wasn't it, up on the hill?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I guess so. She said, well, they was nine and ten and seven or so. And it was a stormy day in March, you know, kind of snowing. And this old lady made them play outdoors. And she always tells that story, said that they couldn't stay in the house. They had to stay out in the cold the day I was born.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And she said, well, she was ten.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But they were supposed to be in the house?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, ordinarily they would, but you know, we didn't go to the hospital. My mother was in labor there and I suppose that had to keep the kids away. I guess they don't know from the day that it's conceived anymore, you know, like they do now, but be no surprise, I suppose, to them kids. It sure changed in that way. Don't you find it that way?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Now the kids, they just know.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: More, I think, a little. That's what the father, the mother told her husband, had to tell this boy about the birds and the bees. And so he got him off one time and he said, it's time for you to know about the birds and talk about the birds and the bees. And the kid says, what do you want to know, Dad?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And that's about the way it is, you know, they know everything.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there very much in Viola when you were young? What stores were there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, at one time we've had about three stores in Viola, at one time. Anyway, two pretty good, so there's a little competition. But the first store I remember was when I told you we had the big hotel. And now that was when lumber was cheap because nobody now would build a hotel. But of course there wouldn't be any call for it because they would stay in Moscow. But then, you know, that there wasn't so many places to stay in the traveling man would to stay at this hotel. And that hotel served meals and it had place rooms to stay.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And it had a little store too, don't you remember Comer's store?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I can't remember exactly what he had, but I suppose a little candy and flour and nuts and bolts and what have you. Just ordinary little store. Now it seems like they had everything. If you had a store, you had a little of everything. Now it's specialized, you know, like the Safeway and them. There's one thing that they sell. So, and that was around when you were young. Yeah. I expect we stayed there. I said a couple or three winters when I first started school because that seemed like a long ways. And that's how deep the snow was, you know. Our dad would come a Friday afternoon and get us and bring us back. And you know now it's five minutes even to walk. I don't know how it got so close.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, how far away were you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Three miles. But the roads were awful bad. You would get awful bad in the breakup, you know, after it snowed all winter and then the slush and things.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I can remember in the even about the mud yet, can't you? Would you get stuck in it much?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, you could easily up where we lived when they first begun to haul big loads over the mountain, we call it. Why then there's somebody always stuck, you know. Couldn't get up the hill one way or the other, you know. Admire down. I can remember Burt Morgan pulling him out many times. That was after I was grown up when he'd have a load of feed or something going back to the hill. The hill where Eva or Eva lived, that hill was on his band too. It drifted. That drifted there, but on up to the big mountain where we go over. You see, this is Farm Isle here and over the hill is Flanagan Creek. This is always confused in everybody's mind. They call it going up Flanagan Creek, but it isn't Flanagan Creek until they get over the mountain. But everybody knows where Flanagan Creek is.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So what was the story about how you went to school then? You'd come in here? You'd come to Viola?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: There was school at Viola here and that's when we stayed at the hotel. That's when I was little. And then later, the other girls were going to Moscow and it used to be one or two winters. You batched, you had rooms, and sometimes you worked for your board. You worked for your board once time at Mrs. Smith's and just the dressmakers. Well, they'd sometimes come home on Friday night and I can remember it was awful to get up early Monday morning to drive with the team. Our dad would take us early enough to get to school. Well, I don't remember that too long, but we did that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long would you stay in Viola?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, all we'd stay until... Eighth grade. It used to be in the eighth grade. Yeah, eighth grade. And then later, when Shirley went to school, we had a couple of years of high school, but I never had high school while her I, but when my daughter was there, we did.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But you came into Viola every winter and went to school in here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, that's what we did for tour of three winters. We stayed and then I don't... Well, I guess the kids all got big enough to go to town to school. Everybody didn't do that, but I remember Vanna Cheney said she cooked for the kids over here and they live three miles up on another hill. So, I guess it was too far to come every day. You just couldn't get here.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, it just called them rag rugs that you make the strips and sew them together and then use the warp and make the rag rug. I guess that... That was the kind of rug that you... Seemed to be. I don't know what they look like. You don't?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No. Well, we don't have any. Oh wait, maybe I do. Okay. Yeah, I think probably you do. They make them in strips and they're sewed strips together, old-fashioned, I would say. Well, I suppose the loom would be so wide and then if you wanted it wider you put two or three strips and sew them together. I think that's probably. I want to...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Have you told me again about the 93 depression? What do you remember about it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Ninety... I'm about to... They still had to burn wood, so he always said that was a pretty good year for him. He built the new house because he could trade wood for everything. He had a few cattle and they'd bring loads of grain. It was damaged, you know, but it was still a good cattle feed. Yeah, that was a pretty bad year. A lot of people mortgaged their homes and everything them days, but I guess our dad didn't have to do that. He sold wood, but it was hard times for quite a few years after that because he didn't have much money, you know. And we used to have the land banks and I guess there's land banks yet, but I don't know anything about them, but so many people I can remember years after were still paying for their places because they mortgaged them to live during the depression. But I guess we've had pretty bad depression since then, haven't we?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Twenty-nine, don't they talk of twenty-nine, when a lot of people lost their money. I never had any to lose, but I guess a lot of people did. But I remember when the bank went broke and plus, our dad lost all the money he had. It wasn't four or five hundred dollars, but the bank went broke and never did get. Don't think they ever got anything. Usually you get about ten cents on the dollar after, and maybe he did, maybe I just don't remember. I wasn't interested then. I was just a school kid. Probably money didn't mean too much to me. Oh, it went broke.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When did it go broke? You were pretty young then?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yeah, in Palouse. I was about, oh, probably twelve or thirteen. Do you remember that?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No, I remember. Yes, he had a little money. Well, I don't know. Just why. But I can remember when him and Dan had that thrashing machine, or Dan did, and I guess he put up money for it to run it or something. I remember that money that they made was in the bank and Palouse and never got anything for it. But I can remember the banks in Moscow closed doors too during some time. But that was well, but I don't think that would have been twenty-nine when the Palouse went broke. I don't know what made it go broke.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever, did you work out in the fields much?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, I helped my dad, but never, and then there's shock hay and things like that. You know, when we had alfalfa, everybody would get out and put up the shocks and the hay.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers:
words: But later then, in later years, Moscow, after they begun to raise peas, Moscow had, have you heard this story before?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: You told it to me last time. Had about four pea houses, and everybody that could get away from home picked peas. And that was big business. That's the first time I ever remember women working out much. Everybody picked peas in the winter. They called it a pick and peas, but they didn't pick them. Picked out the culls, but they always called it pick and peas. But the, oh, this, not an elevator, what things, roller things come towards you, and you just picked out the cull peas as it went by. Put the cull peas in the box, and your good peas went down in the hopper. But some people, well, I believe at Wilson's there was probably two shifts, and I expect 40 at a shift, or maybe 50. So it took a lot of women. There was all women. Yeah, the men took care of the machinery, but I don't remember any men starting peas.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did any women work in the prune dryer and stuff that they had here?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, we never had a big prune dryer, but the Chaney's had a dryer that they dried all the prunes for years, and then later they got to shipping them. They didn't dry them anymore. I guess it wasn't sale or something. They got a sale for them fresh. And we kids used to pick prunes in the fall of the Chaney Archer, and I have picked apples, not picked apples, packed apples at Juliaetta a couple of falls. You know, there used to be arches around.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When was that that you'd go down there? Were you married?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No, just before I was married, and they begun early enough that you could work six weeks before school started. And I think they picked peas, packed fruit here some fall. Everybody had big arches.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How would you pack fruit down there?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, well, they were sorted, and you wrapped them, you got pretty fast. You wrapped them in paper.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Each one you get wrapped up?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Mm-hmm. And according to the amount in the box, great big apples. You'd only have about 50, and then all the way up to 75 or 60. I just really forgot. But they shipped lots of apples from here. But then after wheat got better fries, well, then everybody dug up their arches and went wheat farming. But...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Last time you told me about, you remember the first week that you saw?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Wheat. Oh, yes, that was when I was a kid, and our father got enough

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: to sow a field of wheat. And old man, Mm-hmm. Oh, what's with the man that Carrie, you know, lived up on the hill with all the boys. He cut it with a scythe. And I remember putting wheat around. You'd gather up a bundle, and you'd take another bundle and fasten the wheat together and put it around, make shocks. I can remember my dad doing that. Of course, there's just a little. And then I think probably in a year or two, we got the binder. But Mr. Wear-eye, wear-eye, cut it with a scythe. I can remember trailing along behind him. I was probably like funny about them. And not enough to thrash, but later then every year my dad had to have a little more in cultivation until it finally got so we had enough to... Well, they bound. And then later years, there was a thrashing machine went through and good thrash. Now, you talked about strawberries last time.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you have strawberries?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yes. Old hillsides. That was got to be a cash crop, too. We kids was all raised in the strawberry patch. I'd have to pick strawberries, but that's the time you could buy a few things. The stores would buy your strawberries, you know. You know what crates are. Now they're flats. But crates used to be double. Had two layers in. And they sold for lots less than the one flap does now. But even so, money went lots farther. You bought lots more things then.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever get any money yourself to just buy whatever you wanted?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, the first time, first money I ever made in the amount, I packed apples. And I think I made about, oh, $300 that fall. I thought I was rich. I bought a rug at home, you know, and I bought me a new saddle for my horse. And I can't remember all the things I bought, but that was a lot of money in them days. I can remember when I went to Creighton's. Creighton's store was in Moscow, yet them days. And I bought things there, and I can remember how that old fella asked my mother what about the money. He didn't think I had it in the bank, but he couldn't believe it. I was about 15. But I think he didn't see that much money too often either, you know. That story was. But that's about the first I remember, having any good money of my own.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What is it they call it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Honest money or mad money is what Anne calls it when her husband dibbies out a little that he don't, she don't have to account for. Yeah, that must.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What did you do between the time that you graduated from school and got married?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, I just got married right after I got out of school. But all these things happened, the picking, apple, working in the fruit. And I went with a cook house, too, a couple of years. That was money before I got married, helping somebody in the cook house. I made money at that, too. But from the time I was 15 to 19, I think it was only 19 when I got married, there were just four years that I worked out to do things and make money. But at home, we had strawberries, but we didn't really get paid. I think we got things that we wanted, but they didn't just pay us then. We just worked at home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, I wanted to ask you, do you know anything about the boy that burned down the schoolhouse?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, indeed I did. Lived over here across from uni all their life, old stacker, and my dad was good friends. And these boys come along. Well, their older girl married one of them, Wirai boys, you remember, that come up. They come from back east, this Wirai family. And I remember then they were male carriers. The boys grew up, and they were pretty hard up, a big family. Didn't have much money. And I know I can remember Bill Wirai telling. Our dad said, I think we just are to death if it hadn't been for you letting us cut wood and have a beef to eat, and probably potatoes. When they come from back east, didn't have any place to come. Well, that family grew up, and they all worked for my dad. But this one married a stacker girl, and that was this boy who did all the burning. That was his older sister. But she must have been quite a bit older than these two boys. You know, there was an adopted girl in between, and I don't know where they got the Maimie.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Didn't their daughter die, and then they adopted one?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Something, maybe. But the older girl was Ella, you know. She married this male carrier all his life. He just died a few years ago and carried a male out of clues.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But what inspired that boy?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: We don't know. He was, nowadays you'd call him a little bit retarded. He was a little fat. They were German people. And there was two boys. What was it? Bernard, and what was the little one's name?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: The one that done the fire wasn't Bernard. Bernard was about my age. And then Maimie was the adopted girl, a little older. Some way they took her when she was little, but she wasn't any relation. But she was their daughter, of course. So Bernard, was he Freddy?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: I don't remember who your name is. Well, anyway, they said he just didn't want to go to school. But why?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: He built the fires. Nobody knew. But afterwards they decided that this stepdaughter and him kind of either dared each other. She was a with him son when he built the fires. But nobody knew that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now what, he burnt down the church?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: He burnt the church and he burnt the school. And then there was an old hall set up here above my son's place, you know, on the corner there, just across the river. It was a shop. And they had the dance hall above and below. Then they fixed up, had school in that. I went to school in that or before that. I guess it had been a little schoolhouse before. But anyway, nobody knew why he burnt that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But every Sunday night we'd have a big fire for three Sundays, I think, wasn't it?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Well, you wasn't here. But finally they found out who it was. They caught him one night. I think this girl and this young boy, he wasn't very old. He couldn't have been more than 13 or 14. And she was about 16, I guess. And they caught him at it. And the father felt so bad. He didn't know they was doing that. And so rather than to, oh, they had a trial and a few things. But rather than to send him away, the father moved away. He sold out over here. And they moved up by Rosalia. And it was funny, many years later, this sister of ours that's up in Spokane, her and her husband used to go up to the lake a lot in Fayshup, Chet Collette. Well, one time she was fishing there. And she said, this man and woman were taking pictures. And she, of course, was out there catching fish. She was a pretty good fisherman. And they said, can't we take your picture?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And she said, yes, if you'll send me one when it's done. So when it come, she lived in Spokane, then her poem, and I don't know, when it come, there was this boy's name on the, that had taken the picture. She didn't know it because she was away from here and married before this happened, you see. But she said there it was a stacker boy's name. But I can't think what his name was. It might have been Freddie, but the oldest one that went to school with me was Bernard. But anyway, so he grew up and married evidently and lived someplace up there. He probably grew into a pretty smart kid. I don't know. They said he was just a, what do they call him, that loved to see fires. And they said maybe that was what ailed him. Maybe he just liked to see a fire. And others said he didn't want to go to school, but I don't think he would have been that desperate. A kid don't usually be that desperate. I can't think he would be even 14 when he built them fires. They don't seem to me like he would have been more than 12. But anyway, he done away with a lot of buildings. He was going a lot of trouble. Yes, he did.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you go to dance this much?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, indeed. After they built, the hall used to set up here. All right, about where Lila lives. You know who Lila is?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: For this nice little house on the hill. Well, hall set up there. During my time, I thought I couldn't hardly have a dance without me, about 15 to 16, 17. And my husband's people, they had a little orchestra for three brothers. And even after we were married, we always went to dances up there.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: They built that place up there after this one burned down?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Yes. When you was young, you went upstairs there that got burnt down. Well, then, Yuna and I have talked about that. We don't know who built this one up here. We didn't have any grainage here then. And we don't know, but I think Bud said the band boys. Some way, somebody engineered and took up a collection of lumber wasn't very high then. They built that. And it was quite a nice big hall. But long ago, when I was little, we had a band. And I can remember the band boys used to march on decoration day. And that was Maccabees, the Maccabee Lodge. But that seemed to dwindle out. I don't know then whatever become. That just faded. I can remember Sam Bowles and that's about all I can remember. But then those others around, whoever was in the band. But I can remember seeing them when their outfits on, do you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And I think that hall must have belonged to them. The first hall that was built.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where with the band?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Old march down around. I don't know. I suppose we went to cemetery. I can't remember. But I can remember seeing them all dolled up. Do you?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: No. I forgot that part.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did you meet your husband? Did you know?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, they farmed out on the other side of Moscow. And then they moved over here. I guess moved to Viola. But I think I knew him in Moscow when we were going to school. And then they moved out here. Now here's another one that went through 93 and lost everything John's dad. Because he had a big, he had a big crop in. I don't think he owned the land, but they rented. And he had a lot of boys to work at. And his brother-in-law, I remember he was always mad at that brother-in-law. He said he was going to thrash for him. But instead of that, he quit on his and went back home and done his. So he saved most of his life. Grandpa Barnes was always mad about that. And you would be, you know, but a fellow looks out for themselves, you know. And the rain come. And my dad always said, you'd think it was going to clear off. And maybe it would stay clear and sunshine for a whole day or two. And just about the time it got ready and dry and after thrash, it would shower again. And that's the way it done all fall. That was 93. I can remember him telling that. Do you or do you?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, do you remember much about the seventh day I've been coming?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Oh, indeed I did. I was there when they come and I've outlived them. I always said that. Yes, I remember when the first one that come that we owe, well, there's some before around that lived here. There was one family, the Christisons. But when they got the clock in, you know, they were going to have a cereal meal. It was going to make breakfast food. And they got lots of people coming. That was the first. I suppose they were the field man. And they had a little money. And then the ones that come later, why, they were looking for work and they didn't materialize, you know. And so then they just went away one by one. Oh, well, they bought little places and probably would work at the meals or something. But, and then they had the school. And that brought in a few boarding students up here where the Shefflins live. Yes, we outlived them all. Mr. Hart come, I can remember he was going to buy our place. And our folks set up all night. I wondered just where they'd go or what they'd do. But by morning, now I was about 15, 14, 13, some. I was the only kid at home because I can remember Moss saying the Advent women thought that's the only kid she had. The rest was all grown up and married. And I was riding a horse. But the Hart then, he went over and bought Mrs. Roth's place and built that house. And my folks stayed right there. They decided they didn't want to sell. So then they bought all around us. And our dad sold some of the land down there. You know, somebody bought, stripped down there and built a house. But the tent went back, couldn't pay for it. And so that's the way it was. But there was houses all around about like there is trailer houses now. The lumber was cheap and everybody buy a little five acres or so. And there was several houses around there. And they all went to school. I guess they had a good school. The teachers would come from Walla Walla College place. College place is an Advent town.

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: And that's Karen, you know who Karen is. Well, Karen's second generation of the Advent family. But I don't think she's Advent. But her folks were strong Advents and her dad still is and her grandma.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there ever bad feelings between people who were Adventists?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Adventists and other, no, because we always had church up here. And that was just Christian church or community. And the Advent pretty much stayed away. They didn't send their kids down here. We was too early. But in later years they did. Karen's dad went to school with my daughter. And they got lots broader minded afterwards. They thought the world was just coming to an end. They used to preach and have camp meeting. They had a great big tent over there. Huskies used to go and good ministers come. But they still thought they would end up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where were the camp meetings?

speaker: Jennie Cuthbert Brouillard or Fannie Byers
words: Right here in Viola, several of them. Right, yeah. One up there by the gray house before that house was built. I can remember going to camp meeting there. Big tent. Those were seventh day. That's before then in later years these faith people, you know, the one that Dorothy belongs to. She, they had camp meetings here when they first started out.
